text: This patient is a fifty-six year old male who presented with left plank pain and recurrent history of UTI and CT scan revealed it was a pyonephrotic kidney on the left side. So first PCN was done and after stabilization, patient was planned for nephrectomy because the DTP scan showed it is a non-functional kidney.

The surgeon encounters extensive adhesions within the abdominal cavity during the nephrectomy. Gentle dissection reveals the renal vessels, including what appears to be the renal vein, amidst the adhesions near the kidney and bowel.
The surgeon dissects the renal vein, clips it with Hem-o-lok clips, and divides it. Subsequently, the renal artery is identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon proceeds with posterior dissection of the kidney, which appears highly adherent to the abdominal wall, likely due to previous surgeries and scarring. The laparoscopic approach is maintained until an unexpected finding occurs.
The surgeon discovers a gauze piece wrapped around the kidney, likely left from an open pylorotomy performed twenty-two years ago, contributing to recurrent infections and perinephric collection.

The surgeon attempts to dissect dense adhesions around the kidney using various energy sources. Initially, a harmonic scalpel is used, but due to its ineffectiveness, the surgeon switches to electrocautery and hooks, resulting in visible charring of the tissue.

The surgeon carefully divides the gauze piece adhered to the kidney and removes the specimen. Remaining gauze attached to the abdominal wall is meticulously dissected and removed using a harmonic scalpel.

It's a very rare thing that a patient with gauzy fibroma or forgotten gauze piece will present after twenty two years and it got undetected on multiple ultrasounds as well as CT scan. It was an incidental and surprise finding during the operation that we got.
